  c(1334, 200118596, 200019634)

# Scan2 from Kid 1

pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$scanID == 297, c("family", "father", "mother")] <-
  c(1334, 200118596, 200019634)

# Scan1 from Kid 2

pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$scanID == 298, c("family", "father", "mother")] <-
  c(1344, 200116780, 200005043)

# Scan2 from Kid 2

pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$scanID == 299, c("family", "father", "mother")] <-
  c(1344, 200116780, 200005043)

pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$family == 1334, ]
##    scanID subjectID family    father    mother CoriellID race sex status
## 17    296 200016815   1334 200118596 200019634   NA10846  CEU   M      0
## 18    297 200016815   1334 200118596 200019634   NA10846  CEU   M      0
## 42    321 200118596   1334         0         0   NA12144  CEU   M      1
## 43    322 200118596   1334         0         0   NA12144  CEU   M      1
## 44    323 200019634   1334         0         0   NA12145  CEU   F      0
## 45    324 200019634   1334         0         0   NA12145  CEU   F      0
##                                 genoRunID          plate batch
## 17 WG1001172-DNAH05-CIDR_10846@1007850610 WG0063165-AMP2     C
## 18 WG1000991-DNAH04-CIDR_10846@1007850376 WG0058565-AMP2     B
## 42 WG1001172-DNAA08-CIDR_12144@1007850613 WG0063166-AMP2     C
## 43 WG1000991-DNAA07-CIDR_12144@1007850412 WG0058566-AMP2     A
## 44 WG1000991-DNAB10-CIDR_12145@1007850436 WG0058567-AMP2     B
## 45 WG1001172-DNAB11-CIDR_12145@1007850682 WG0063167-AMP2     C
##                       file  missing.e1   missing.e2     het.A       het.X
## 17 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_77.csv 0.003810734 0.0006462036 0.2903226 0.000000000
## 18 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_27.csv 0.007303906 0.0035541195 0.2905508 0.001057082
## 42 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_74.csv 0.000000000 0.0000000000 0.3006012 0.000000000
## 43 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_28.csv 0.007939028 0.0071082391 0.3022200 0.003191489
## 44 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_68.csv 0.001931123 0.0006544503 0.3010537 0.264488936
## 45 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_75.csv 0.024782749 0.0242146597 0.2988150 0.272335845
##    sex_changed
## 17           M
## 18           M
## 42           M
## 43           M
## 44           F
## 45           F
pData(scanAnnot)[pData(scanAnnot)$family == 1344, ]
##    scanID subjectID family    father    mother CoriellID race sex status
## 19    298 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 20    299 200071490   1344 200116780 200005043   NA10851  CEU   M      0
## 38    317 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 39    318 200116780   1344         0         0   NA12056  CEU   M      1
## 40    319 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
## 41    320 200005043   1344         0         0   NA12057  CEU   F      1
##                                 genoRunID          plate batch
## 19 WG1000972-DNAB01-CIDR_10851@1007850566 WG0060472-AMP2     B
## 20 WG1000971-DNAA01-CIDR_10851@1007850374 WG0061537-AMP2     C
## 38 WG1000971-DNAB04-CIDR_12056@1007850430 WG0061538-AMP2     C
## 39 WG1000972-DNAC04-CIDR_12056@1007850435 WG0060473-AMP2     C
## 40 WG1000971-DNAC07-CIDR_12057@1007850454 WG0061539-AMP2     A
## 41 WG1000972-DNAD07-CIDR_12057@1007850405 WG0060474-AMP2     B
##                       file   missing.e1   missing.e2     het.A     het.X
## 19  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_8.csv 0.0022229279 0.0003231018 0.3028629 0.0000000
## 20  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_6.csv 0.0136551286 0.0109854604 0.3018584 0.0000000
## 38  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_7.csv 0.0012702445 0.0012924071 0.2597793 0.0000000
## 39  GENEVA_1M_HapMap_9.csv 0.0006351223 0.0006462036 0.2601504 0.0000000
## 40 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_62.csv 0.0009655616 0.0006544503 0.3159478 0.2736842
## 41 GENEVA_1M_HapMap_63.csv 0.0022529771 0.0009816754 0.3155779 0.2736842
##    sex_changed
## 19           M
## 20           M
## 38           M
## 39           M
## 40           F
## 41           F

Now that we’ve “corrected” the sample-swaps, we should make a new IBD plot to verify that observed and expected relationships now match.

#   Recalculate the relatedness as above

gds_geno <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_geno.gds", package = "GWASdata")
snps_to_include_for_kinship <-
  snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n1 < 0.05]
gdsobj <- snpgdsOpen(gds_geno)
ibdobj <- snpgdsIBDKING(gdsobj, snp.id = snps_to_include_for_kinship)
## IBD analysis (KING method of moment) on genotypes:
## Excluding 1,339 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 35 SNPs (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: NaN, missing rate: NaN)
##     # of samples: 77
##     # of SNPs: 1,926
##     using 1 thread
## No family is specified, and all individuals are treated as singletons.
## Relationship inference in the presence of population stratification.
## KING IBD:    the sum of all selected genotypes (0,1,2) = 132998
## CPU capabilities: Double-Precision SSE2
## Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025    (internal increment: 65536)
## [..................................................]  0%, ETC: ---        [==================================================] 100%, completed, 0s
## Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025    Done.
snpgdsClose(gdsobj)

#   Re-draw the pedigree

ped <-
  pData(scanAnnot)[, c("family", "subjectID", "father", "mother", "sex_changed")]
dim(ped)
## [1] 77  5
names(ped) <- c("family", "individ", "father", "mother", "sex")
head(ped)
##   family   individ    father    mother sex
## 1   1341 200191449         0         0   F
## 2   1341 200191449         0         0   F
## 3   1341 200030290 200099417 200191449   F
## 4   1341 200030290 200099417 200191449   F
## 5   1341 200099417         0         0   M
## 6   1341 200099417         0         0   M
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $duplicates
##    family   individ copies match
## 1    1341 200191449      2  TRUE
## 2    1341 200030290      2  TRUE
## 3    1341 200099417      2  TRUE
## 4    1341 200015835      2  TRUE
## 5    1340 200099636      2  TRUE
## 6    1340 200076256      2  TRUE
## 7    1408 200074814      2  TRUE
## 8    1408 200094287      2  TRUE
## 9    1408 200019401      2  TRUE
## 10   1334 200016815      2  TRUE
## 11   1334 200118596      2  TRUE
## 12   1334 200019634      2  TRUE
## 13   1344 200071490      2  TRUE
## 14   1344 200116780      2  TRUE
## 15   1344 200005043      2  TRUE
## 16   1347 200121301      2  TRUE
## 17   1347 200009887      2  TRUE
## 18   1347 200044196      2  TRUE
## 19   1362 200024383      2  TRUE
## 20   1362 200187448      2  TRUE
## 21   1362 200169440      2  TRUE
## 22   1362 200018192      2  TRUE
## 23   1362 200118709      2  TRUE
## 24      4 200047857      2  TRUE
## 25      4 200073630      2  TRUE
## 26      5 200102386      2  TRUE
## 27      5 200079101      2  TRUE
## 28      5 200022488      2  TRUE
## 29      9 200066330      2  TRUE
## 30      9 200033736      2  TRUE
## 31     12 200066777      2  TRUE
## 32     12 200160551      2  TRUE
## 33     28 200003216      2  TRUE
## 34     28 200034659      2  TRUE
## 
## $parent.no.individ.entry
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1      13   1341           mother 200039107
## 2      14   1341           mother 200039107
## 
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
#   Remove duplicates, as before

dups <- chk$duplicates
head(dups)
##   family   individ copies match
## 1   1341 200191449      2  TRUE
## 2   1341 200030290      2  TRUE
## 3   1341 200099417      2  TRUE
## 4   1341 200015835      2  TRUE
## 5   1340 200099636      2  TRUE
## 6   1340 200076256      2  TRUE
uni.ped <- pedigreeDeleteDuplicates(ped, dups)
dim(uni.ped)
## [1] 43  5
chk <- pedigreeCheck(uni.ped)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $parent.no.individ.entry
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1       8   1341           mother 200039107
## 
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
## 
## $subfamilies.ident
##   family subfamily   individ
## 1   1362         1 200003297
## 2   1362         1 200169440
## 3   1362         1 200187448
## 6   1362         2 200018192
## 4   1362         2 200024383
## 5   1362         2 200118709
#   Recall there was a person whose mother needed to be added to the pedigree

ni <- chk$parent.no.individ.entry
ni
##   row.num family no_individ_entry  parentID
## 1       8   1341           mother 200039107
parent <-
  data.frame(
    family = ni$family,
    individ = ni$parentID,
    father = 0,
    mother = 0,
    sex = "F",
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
ped.updated <- rbind(uni.ped, parent)
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.updated)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
## 
## $subfamilies.ident
##    family subfamily   individ
## 2    1341         1 200030290
## 3    1341         1 200099417
## 1    1341         1 200191449
## 5    1341         2 200015835
## 6    1341         2 200039107
## 4    1341         2 200122600
## 7    1362         1 200003297
## 8    1362         1 200169440
## 9    1362         1 200187448
## 12   1362         2 200018192
## 10   1362         2 200024383
## 11   1362         2 200118709
#   Recall that a family had to be broken into two new sub-families

ped.complete <- ped.updated
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
subf <- chk$subfamilies.ident
subf
##    family subfamily   individ
## 2    1341         1 200030290
## 3    1341         1 200099417
## 1    1341         1 200191449
## 5    1341         2 200015835
## 6    1341         2 200039107
## 4    1341         2 200122600
## 7    1362         1 200003297
## 8    1362         1 200169440
## 9    1362         1 200187448
## 12   1362         2 200018192
## 10   1362         2 200024383
## 11   1362         2 200118709
table(subf$family)
## 
## 1341 1362 
##    6    6
subf.ids <- subf$individ[subf$subfamily == 2]
newfam <- ped.complete$individ %in% subf.ids
ped.complete$family[newfam] <-
  paste0(ped.complete$family[newfam], "-2")
table(ped.complete$family)
## 
##     12   1334   1340   1341 1341-2   1344   1347   1362 1362-2   1408     28 
##      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3      3 
##      4      5     58   58-2      9 
##      3      3      1      1      3
pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.complete)
## All row numbers refer to row in the full pedigree (not just within family). 
##  Correct current problems and rerun pedigreeCheck.
## There may be additional problems not investigated because of the current problems.
chk
## $one.person.fams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
#   Earlier we split family 58 into two singleton families (the "father" and
#   "daughter" who weren't actually related) pedigreeCheck doesn't like
#   singletons, so we need to assign dummy parents for the original two members
#   of family 58 And these 4 dummy parents need to be added into the pedigree

onefams <- chk$one.person.fams
onefams
##   family founder
## 1     58    TRUE
## 2   58-2    TRUE
ped.complete[ped.complete$family %in% c("58", "58-2"), ]
##    family   individ father mother sex
## 76     58 200122151      0      0   F
## 77   58-2 200105428      0      0   M
ped.complete[ped.complete$family == "58",  c("father", "mother")] <-
  c("1111", "2222")
ped.complete[ped.complete$family == "58-2", c("father", "mother")] <-
  c("3333", "4444")

parents <- data.frame(
  family = c("58", "58", "58-2", "58-2"),
  individ = c("1111", "2222", "3333", "4444"),
  father = c(0, 0, 0, 0),
  mother = c(0, 0, 0, 0),
  sex = c("M", "F", "M", "F"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

#   Complete the final pedigree, and now pedigreeCheck is happy (it returns NULL)

ped.final <- rbind(ped.complete, parents)
chk <- pedigreeCheck(ped.final)

# Recalculate expected relationships in order to make a new plot, as above

ped.complete <- ped.final
rels <- pedigreePairwiseRelatedness(ped.final)
length(rels$inbred.fam)
## [1] 0
relprs <- rels$relativeprs
relprs[1:5, ]
##    Individ1  Individ2 relation kinship family
## 1 200191449 200030290       PO    0.25   1341
## 2 200191449 200099417        U    0.00   1341
## 3 200030290 200099417       PO    0.25   1341
## 4 200099636 200032162        U    0.00   1340
## 5 200099636 200076256       PO    0.25   1340
table(relprs$relation)
## 
## PO  U 
## 32 16
samp <- pData(scanAnnot)[, c("scanID", "subjectID")]
samp <- samp[match(ibdobj$sample.id, samp$scanID), ]
names(samp) <- c("scanID", "Individ")
ibd <- snpgdsIBDSelection(ibdobj)
ibd <- merge(ibd, samp, by.x = "ID1", by.y = "scanID")
ibd <-
  merge(
    ibd,
    samp,
    by.x = "ID2",
    by.y = "scanID",
    suffixes = c("1", "2")
  )
ibd$ii <- pasteSorted(ibd$Individ1, ibd$Individ2)
relprs$ii <- pasteSorted(relprs$Individ1, relprs$Individ2)
ibd <- merge(ibd, relprs[, c("ii", "relation")], all.x = TRUE)
names(ibd)[names(ibd) == "relation"] <- "exp.rel"
ibd$exp.rel[ibd$Individ1 == ibd$Individ2] <- "Dup"
ibd$exp.rel[is.na(ibd$exp.rel)] <- "U"
table(ibd$exp.rel, useNA = "ifany")
## 
##  Dup   PO    U 
##   34   90 2802
#   Use the ibdAssignRelatednessKing function to call the observed relationships
#   based on IBS0 and kinship values for each pair

ibd$obs.rel <- ibdAssignRelatednessKing(ibd$IBS0, ibd$kinship)
table(ibd$obs.rel, useNA = "ifany")
## 
## Deg3  Dup   PO    U 
##   64   34   90 2738
#   Observe that now the observed and expected relationships match (if we take
#   Deg3 and Unrelated as synonymous)

table(ibd$exp.rel, ibd$obs.rel)
##      
##       Deg3  Dup   PO    U
##   Dup    0   34    0    0
##   PO     0    0   90    0
##   U     64    0    0 2738
#   Finally, plot KC vs IBS0

#   Draw horizontal hresholds for assigning relationships using kinship
#   coefficients (taken from table 1 of Manichaikul (2010))

#   Now none of  the dots appear to be in the "wrong" clusters

cut.dup <- 1 / (2 ^ (3 / 2))
cut.deg1 <- 1 / (2 ^ (5 / 2))
cut.deg2 <- 1 / (2 ^ (7 / 2))
cut.deg3 <- 1 / (2 ^ (9 / 2))
cols <- c(Dup = "magenta",
          PO = "cyan",
          U = "black")
plot(ibd$IBS0,
     ibd$kinship,
     col = cols[ibd$exp.rel],
     xlab = "Fraction of IBS=0",
     ylab = "Kinship coefficient")
abline(
  h = c(cut.deg1, cut.deg2, cut.deg3, cut.dup),
  lty = 2,
  col = "gray"
)
legend("topright",
       legend = names(cols),
       col = cols,
       pch = 1)

Part 4 - Report (Relatedness)

  • Summarize the pedigree structures you observe in the data.

  • Describe what discrepancies you observe in the initial kinship vs IBS0 plot.

  • Explain exactly the kinds of swaps/discrepancies that were found, which IDs were involved, and exactly how you “fixed” them.

  • How would you recommend following up on these discrepancies “in real life” (instead of just assuming we know what happened and manually changing the data, which is a bad idea)?

  • Add a column to scanAnnot called “relationship.changes”, which briefly summarizes these “fixes” (add metadata for the column, too).

#   <Your code goes here>
#Part-4 Qn-1 : Summarize the pedigree structures you observe in the data.
#Ans : There are none inbred individuals(and family) present.
pedigreePairwiseRelatedness(ped.final)
## $inbred.fam
## NULL
## 
## $inbred.KC
## NULL
## 
## $relativeprs
##     Individ1  Individ2 relation kinship family
## 1  200191449 200030290       PO    0.25   1341
## 2  200191449 200099417        U    0.00   1341
## 3  200030290 200099417       PO    0.25   1341
## 4  200099636 200032162        U    0.00   1340
## 5  200099636 200076256       PO    0.25   1340
## 6  200032162 200076256       PO    0.25   1340
## 7  200122600 200015835       PO    0.25 1341-2
## 8  200122600 200039107        U    0.00 1341-2
## 9  200015835 200039107       PO    0.25 1341-2
## 10 200074814 200094287       PO    0.25   1408
## 11 200074814 200019401       PO    0.25   1408
## 12 200094287 200019401        U    0.00   1408
## 13 200016815 200118596       PO    0.25   1334
## 14 200016815 200019634       PO    0.25   1334
## 15 200118596 200019634        U    0.00   1334
## 16 200071490 200116780       PO    0.25   1344
## 17 200071490 200005043       PO    0.25   1344
## 18 200116780 200005043        U    0.00   1344
## 19 200121301 200009887       PO    0.25   1347
## 20 200121301 200044196       PO    0.25   1347
## 21 200009887 200044196        U    0.00   1347
## 22 200003297 200187448       PO    0.25   1362
## 23 200003297 200169440       PO    0.25   1362
## 24 200187448 200169440        U    0.00   1362
## 25 200024383 200018192       PO    0.25 1362-2
## 26 200024383 200118709       PO    0.25 1362-2
## 27 200018192 200118709        U    0.00 1362-2
## 28 200047857 200063449       PO    0.25      4
## 29 200047857 200073630       PO    0.25      4
## 30 200063449 200073630        U    0.00      4
## 31 200102386 200079101       PO    0.25      5
## 32 200102386 200022488       PO    0.25      5
## 33 200079101 200022488        U    0.00      5
## 34 200066330 200140995       PO    0.25      9
## 35 200066330 200033736       PO    0.25      9
## 36 200140995 200033736        U    0.00      9
## 37 200066777 200160551        U    0.00     12
## 38 200066777 200013233       PO    0.25     12
## 39 200160551 200013233       PO    0.25     12
## 40 200150062 200003216        U    0.00     28
## 41 200150062 200034659       PO    0.25     28
## 42 200003216 200034659       PO    0.25     28
## 43 200122151      1111       PO    0.25     58
## 44 200122151      2222       PO    0.25     58
## 45      1111      2222        U    0.00     58
## 46 200105428      3333       PO    0.25   58-2
## 47 200105428      4444       PO    0.25   58-2
## 48      3333      4444        U    0.00   58-2
#Part-4 Qn-2 : Describe what discrepancies you observe in the initial kinship vs IBS0 plot.
#Ans : The unrelated cluster are supposed to be near zero end or negative kinship values and more than 0.5 IBS values but some are drifting closer to regions expected for related individuals. This could indicate cryptic relatedness or sample mislabeling. The Parent-Offspring cluster should be around 0.25 kinship and 0 ibs but we can see that there are several data points closing on full-sibling and few even on half-siblings,Avuncular, grandparents-grandchildren relation.

#Part-4 Qn-3 : Explain exactly the kinds of swaps/discrepancies that were found, which IDs were involved, and exactly how you "fixed" them.
#Ans: The major ones were with scanID 355(subject ID 200122151) and 356(subject ID 200105428) who were unrelated but were reported as Parent-offspring. This was fixed by making a new family 58-2 for scan ID 356(subject 200105428)
#Another issue was with scan IDs 296 (subject ID 200016815) and 297 (subject ID 200005043) being incorrectly assigned, leading to discrepancies in expected relationships. These individuals were reported as Parent-Offspring (PO) with others but were observed as Unrelated (U), and vice versa. Similarly, scan IDs 298 (subject ID 200071490) and 299 (subject ID 200019634) had incorrect kinship assignments. This was solved by swapping their family ID, father ID and mother ID.

#Part-4 Qn-4 :How would you recommend following up on these discrepancies "in real life" (instead of just assuming we know what happened and manually changing the data, which is a *bad idea*)?

#Part-4 Qn-5 : Add a column to scanAnnot called "relationship.changes", which briefly summarizes these "fixes" (add metadata for the column, too).

Part 5 - Principal component analysis (PCA) and population substructure

Here you’ll be introduced to performing PCA to uncover population substructure.

#   For input when we calculate PCA, we want to exclude SNPs from a few
#   problematic loci

#   Including SNPs from 2q21 (LCT), HLA, 8p23, and 17q21.31 can result PCs that
#   are highly correlated with these SNPs, which is undesirable

#   GWASTools supplies the positions of these loci in the relevant genome build
#   in a data frame called pcaSnpFilters.hg18

#   (Note that these loci are on chromosomes 2, 6, 8, and 17, which aren't
#   included in our genotyping data; we'll proceed in this example as if we had
#   genome-wide SNPs)

filt <- get(data(pcaSnpFilters.hg18))

#   Next we pull out the chromosomes, positions, and IDs of the SNPs in our
#   dataset

chrom <- getChromosome(snpAnnot)
pos <- getPosition(snpAnnot)
snpID <- getSnpID(snpAnnot)
snp.filt <- rep(TRUE, length(snpID))

#   We loop over the 4 regions to be excluded and identify which of our SNPs lie
#   with the endpoints of each

for (f in 1:nrow(filt)) {
  snp.filt[chrom == filt$chrom[f] &
             filt$start.base[f] < pos & pos < filt$end.base[f]] <- FALSE
}

#   Make a vector called snp.sel containing the snpIDs of SNPs not in the
#   problematic regions; these are the SNPs we'll retain for PCA

snp.sel <- snpID[snp.filt]
length(snp.sel)
## [1] 3300
#   (None of our 3300 SNPs get filtered out in this step, since they're not on
#   chromosomes 2, 6, 8, or 17)


### We also want to filter out some samples before running PCA

### Specifically, we shouldn't include duplicates (for subjects with multiple
### samples, we'll just pick one sample)

#   We'll re-order scanAnnot by missing.e1 so duplicate subjectIDs with a higher
#   missing rate are marked as duplicates

#   It's important that scanAnnot stays in the same order when we're done

#   Before changing the order, verify that scanAnnot is ordered by increasing
#   scanID (it is)

allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, sort(scanAnnot$scanID))
## [1] TRUE
#   Now re-order so that duplicate subjectIDs with a higher missing rate are
#   marked as duplicates

scanAnnot <-
  scanAnnot[order(scanAnnot$subjectID, scanAnnot$missing.e1), ]

#   Add a new column called "duplicated" indicating who the duplicates are

scanAnnot$duplicated <- duplicated(scanAnnot$subjectID)
table(scanAnnot$duplicated, useNA = "ifany")
## 
## FALSE  TRUE 
##    43    34
#   Put scanAnnot back in scanID order; this is very important!

scanAnnot <- scanAnnot[order(scanAnnot$scanID), ]
allequal(scanAnnot$scanID, sort(scanAnnot$scanID))
## [1] TRUE
#   Update the metadata

varMetadata(scanAnnot)["duplicated", "labelDescription"] <-
  "TRUE for duplicate scan with higher missing.e1"

#   Now make a vector of the scanIDs, leaving out the duplicates with higher
#   missingness (43 remain)

sample.sel <- scanAnnot$scanID[!scanAnnot$duplicated]
length(sample.sel)
## [1] 43
#   Note that in some applications you would only want to include unrelated
#   subjects when calculating PCs

#   In this exploratory example, we'll include parents and their children
#   instead of restricting ourselves to unrelated "founders""


### Now we can perform LD pruning, where independent SNPs are chosen using a
### "sliding window" along the chromosomes

### Re-open the genotypes (after making sure the previous connection is closed)

#close(genoData)
showfile.gds(closeall = T)
##                                                                                  FileName
## 1 /ihome/crc/install/gcc-12.2.0/r/4.4.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_qxy.gds
## 2  /ihome/crc/install/gcc-12.2.0/r/4.4.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_bl.gds
##   ReadOnly  State
## 1     TRUE closed
## 2     TRUE closed
gdsobj <- snpgdsOpen(genofile)

### Use the snpgdsLDpruning function

#   · sample.id/snp.id are used to retain only the samples/SNPs we picked out
#     above
#   · only autosomal SNPs are used
#   · a MAF threshold of 5% is applied
#   · the maximum window size we use is 1 megabase
#   · the LD threshold is set

#   Read the Details section of the help page for snpgdsLDpruning to understand
#   how the pruning algorithm works

snpset <- snpgdsLDpruning(
  gdsobj,
  sample.id = sample.sel,
  snp.id = snp.sel,
  autosome.only = TRUE,
  maf = 0.05,
  missing.rate = 0.05,
  method = "corr",
  slide.max.bp = 10e6,
  ld.threshold = sqrt(0.1)
)
## SNP pruning based on LD:
## Excluding 1,300 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 237 SNPs (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: 0.05, missing rate: 0.05)
##     # of samples: 43
##     # of SNPs: 1,763
##     using 1 thread
##     sliding window: 10,000,000 basepairs, Inf SNPs
##     |LD| threshold: 0.316228
##     method: correlation
## Chrom 21: |====================|====================|
##     8.80%, 88 / 1,000 (Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025)
## Chrom 22: |====================|====================|
##     8.80%, 88 / 1,000 (Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025)
## 176 markers are selected in total.
snp.pruned <- unlist(snpset, use.names = FALSE)
length(snp.pruned)
## [1] 176
#   The output is a list the SNPs on each chromosome

#   (Roughly 180 SNPs are retained. Note that this procedure includes a random
#   process, so the number of SNPs selected will vary)


#   Now we calculate the PCs by applying snpgdsPCA, using only the pruned SNPs
#   and the samples selected earlier

pca <- snpgdsPCA(gdsobj, sample.id = sample.sel, snp.id = snp.pruned)
## Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on genotypes:
## Excluding 3,124 SNPs (non-autosomes or non-selection)
## Excluding 0 SNP (monomorphic: TRUE, MAF: NaN, missing rate: NaN)
##     # of samples: 43
##     # of SNPs: 176
##     using 1 thread
##     # of principal components: 32
## PCA:    the sum of all selected genotypes (0,1,2) = 6500
## CPU capabilities: Double-Precision SSE2
## Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025    (internal increment: 55532)
## [..................................................]  0%, ETC: ---        [==================================================] 100%, completed, 0s
## Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025    Begin (eigenvalues and eigenvectors)
## Mon Feb 10 00:07:25 2025    Done.
dim(pca$eigenvect)
## [1] 43 32
head(pca$eigenvect)
##            [,1]        [,2]        [,3]        [,4]         [,5]         [,6]
## [1,] -0.1521716  0.09023622 -0.18414086  0.05309719  0.122285812 -0.141410280
## [2,] -0.1655607  0.06569718 -0.19772275  0.06135849  0.061511209  0.011104272
## [3,] -0.1192036 -0.05285129 -0.07068497 -0.03384636  0.002231985  0.179185926
## [4,] -0.1766207  0.18168271 -0.01872539 -0.27338983  0.128413472  0.002954966
## [5,] -0.0606692 -0.01056634 -0.15996805 -0.08607384 -0.001385329 -0.021947230
## [6,] -0.1450841  0.22589175 -0.18174331 -0.25440207 -0.051806797 -0.019796848
##            [,7]         [,8]        [,9]       [,10]        [,11]       [,12]
## [1,] -0.2577066  0.095132012 -0.04972506 -0.03731831  0.123886822  0.23205440
## [2,] -0.3581743  0.129853303  0.01189887 -0.05455642  0.024364538  0.21928822
## [3,] -0.2351196 -0.050492908  0.08691783 -0.07309874 -0.025298466  0.12229870
## [4,] -0.2303294  0.012409903 -0.06817550  0.17544156 -0.225833418 -0.26742199
## [5,] -0.1355734 -0.126816892 -0.02188193  0.18331395  0.123298916  0.07941466
## [6,] -0.1677908 -0.009353708 -0.04217029  0.18283083  0.007614429 -0.10229980
##            [,13]      [,14]       [,15]        [,16]       [,17]       [,18]
## [1,]  0.13610311 -0.1165899  0.22075407  0.002684068 -0.07818919 -0.10528828
## [2,]  0.10377385 -0.1313621  0.17833110 -0.099099400 -0.12147727 -0.21150965
## [3,] -0.08456123 -0.1268529  0.02914832 -0.107088778  0.02600868 -0.31157940
## [4,] -0.08738864  0.1006535 -0.13245430 -0.052874133  0.03598578  0.28912874
## [5,]  0.13143876  0.1657167 -0.10753137 -0.091711352  0.26485587  0.01097379
## [6,]  0.03680542  0.1700786 -0.17334356 -0.046317200  0.19395607  0.14110116
##             [,19]       [,20]       [,21]       [,22]       [,23]        [,24]
## [1,]  0.135400731  0.03412152  0.12273665 -0.14866213  0.31460885  0.254844086
## [2,] -0.069072844 -0.16939781  0.01076859  0.05339847  0.01727602 -0.002635497
## [3,]  0.006519313 -0.11823031 -0.03536881  0.27141485 -0.19895430 -0.259305651
## [4,] -0.105609369 -0.08333043  0.09546604  0.02872618  0.34632958 -0.280310557
## [5,]  0.087420120  0.10213442 -0.32283269  0.11117038 -0.36659680  0.338732874
## [6,] -0.035962880 -0.12088356 -0.10120664  0.07927195 -0.15628238  0.045362411
##            [,25]       [,26]       [,27]       [,28]       [,29]        [,30]
## [1,]  0.32245393 -0.11755497 -0.08809224  0.16607693  0.28436227  0.026405379
## [2,] -0.01605311 -0.07062106  0.09226180 -0.03359912 -0.22527233  0.019863753
## [3,] -0.33721577  0.10727909  0.35823254 -0.12798816  0.14463945 -0.068744435
## [4,]  0.10707238 -0.06931970  0.15776190 -0.01678832  0.01193378  0.006786277
## [5,]  0.01138801  0.19284621 -0.19958087 -0.03184629  0.17350471  0.136187523
## [6,]  0.17819860 -0.03440261 -0.01369724 -0.07648314 -0.19166066 -0.087040177
##            [,31]       [,32]
## [1,] -0.16367839  0.23169953
## [2,]  0.20959417 -0.39049256
## [3,] -0.25321886  0.16838727
## [4,] -0.15055694  0.16078566
## [5,] -0.07997012  0.09816433
## [6,]  0.19529009 -0.14458648
#   The function returns a list of 8 objects, including the eigenvectors and the
#   ordered scanIDs

#   32 eigenvectors/PCs have been provided (in order); they're stored in
#   pca$eigenvect, a 43x32 matrix (43 samples by 32 eigenvectors)

Part 5 - Report

  • How many SNPs were used to calculate PCs?

  • Make pairwise scatterplots of the first 4 PCs, using different plotting symbols for the two populations, as reported in the “race” variable in scanAnnot.

  • Make a parallel coordinate plot for the first 4 plots as well, again distinguishing the plotted lines according to the “race” variable.

  • What information does the first PC appear to convey?

#   <Your code goes here>
#Part-5 Qn-1 : How many SNPs were used to calculate PCs?
#Ans : Around 180 SNPs are being used to calculate PCs.
length(snp.pruned)
## [1] 176
#Part-5 Qn-2 : Make pairwise scatterplots of the first 4 PCs, using different plotting symbols for the two populations, as reported in the "race" variable in scanAnnot.
#Ans : 
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
## Registered S3 method overwritten by 'GGally':
##   method from   
##   +.gg   ggplot2
race_subset <- scanAnnot$race[scanAnnot$scanID %in% sample.sel]
length(race_subset)
## [1] 43
pca_df <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pca$eigenvect[, 1],
  PC2 = pca$eigenvect[, 2],
  PC3 = pca$eigenvect[, 3],
  PC4 = pca$eigenvect[, 4],
  race_subset)

ggpairs(pca_df, columns = 1:4, aes(color = race_subset, shape = race_subset)) +
  theme_minimal()

#Part-5 Qn-3 : Make a parallel coordinate plot for the first 4 plots as well, again distinguishing the plotted lines according to the "race" variable.
#Ans : 
ggparcoord(
  data = pca_df,
  columns = 1:4,  # Select the first 4 PCs
  groupColumn = "race_subset",) +
  labs(title = "Parallel Coordinate Plot of First 4 PCs",
       x = "Principal Components",
       y = "Standardized Values") +
  theme_minimal()

#Part-5 Qn-4 : What information does the first PC appear to convey?

Part 6 - Duplicate Sample Discordance

Here we leverage the duplicate samples to assess genotyping errors. If the same probe often yields different genotypes for duplicate scans from the same subjects, we should probably exclude the genotypes called from that probe. We will use the function duplicateDiscordance.

#   First, we should only use high-quality scans to when we judge the probes

scan.excl <- scanAnnot$scanID[scanAnnot$missing.e1 >= 0.05]
length(scan.excl)
## [1] 0
#   (Recall that none of our scans had high missingness, so we don't have to
#   exclude anyone in this dataset)

#   We also don't want to bother testing for discordance in SNPs that are 100%
#   missing, so we exclude those, too

snp.excl <- snpAnnot$snpID[snpAnnot$missing.n1 == 1]
length(snp.excl)
## [1] 151
#   Get the genotype data (make sure the connection to the file is closed before
#   reopening it)

#close(genoData)
showfile.gds(closeall = T, verbose = T)
##                                                                                   FileName
## 1 /ihome/crc/install/gcc-12.2.0/r/4.4.0/lib64/R/library/GWASdata/extdata/illumina_geno.gds
##   ReadOnly  State
## 1     TRUE closed
genofile <-
  system.file("extdata", "illumina_geno.gds", package = "GWASdata")
genoGDS <- GdsGenotypeReader(genofile)

#   Attach the annotation to it (note that the sex/relatedness information were
#   "fixed" above)

genoData <-
  GenotypeData(genoGDS, snpAnnot = snpAnnot, scanAnnot = scanAnnot)

#   Now we use the dupdisc function to calculate the discordance for each SNP
#   (while excluding any low-quality scans and 100%-missing SNPs)

dupdisc <-
  duplicateDiscordance(
    genoData,
    subjName.col = "subjectID",
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snp.exclude = snp.excl
  )
## subject 1 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 2 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 3 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 4 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 5 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 6 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 7 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 8 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 9 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 10 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 11 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 12 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 13 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 14 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 15 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 16 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 17 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 18 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 19 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 20 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 21 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 22 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 23 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 24 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 25 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 26 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 27 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 28 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 29 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 30 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 31 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 32 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 33 out of 34, 2 replications
## subject 34 out of 34, 2 replications
#   The function returns a list of 3 objects: discordance.by.snp,
#   discordance.by.subject, and correlation.by.subject

names(dupdisc)
## [1] "discordance.by.snp"     "discordance.by.subject" "correlation.by.subject"
dim(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)
## [1] 3149    5
head(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)
##    snpID discordant npair n.disc.subj discord.rate
## 1 999465          0    33           0            0
## 2 999493          0    31           0            0
## 3 999512          0    34           0            0
## 4 999561          0    34           0            0
## 5 999567          0    34           0            0
## 6 999569          0    34           0            0
#   discordance.by.snp has a row for each SNP, detailing the number of
#   duplicate-pairs of scans observed for that SNP, the number of discordant
#   gentoypes observed among those pairs, and the discordance rate

length(dupdisc$discordance.by.subject)
## [1] 34
#   discordance.by.subject is a list of matrices. For each subject with
#   duplicate scans, the matrix shows the discordance between the pairs of scans
#   (i.e., at what proportion of SNPs do two scans from the same person have
#   different genotypes?)

dupdisc$discordance.by.subject[[1]]
##     280 281
## 280   0   0
## 281   0   0
#   For the first subject in dupdisc$discordance.by.subject (scans 280/281), it
#   looks like the genotypes agreed for all the SNPs

dupdisc$discordance.by.subject[[2]]
##              282          283
## 282 0.0000000000 0.0003265839
## 283 0.0003265839 0.0000000000
#   For the second subject (scans 282/283), the discordance rate was
#   0.0003265839


#   Save a summary of this information by merging the discordances into the SNP
#   annotation

snpAnnot$dummy <- 1:nrow(pData(snpAnnot))
pData(snpAnnot) <-
  merge(
    x = pData(snpAnnot),
    y = dupdisc$discordance.by.snp[, c("snpID", "discordant")],
    all.x = T,
    all.y = F
  )

#   Make sure the order of the SNPs is maintained

pData(snpAnnot) <- pData(snpAnnot)[order(snpAnnot$dummy), ]

Part 6 - Report

Briefly explain the purpose of this step and what it means to check for discordant genotypes. Summarize the results. Be sure to answer the following:

  • How many SNPs are discordant in at least one duplicate-pair?

  • What’s the maximum number of discordances seen for any SNP?

  • What’s the median discordance rate? What’s the maximum discordance rate?

  • Decide and explicitly state the threshold, n, for filtering out SNPs based on discordance (i.e., SNPs with dupdisc$discordance.by.snp >= n will be removed)

  • You may find it helpful to call the function duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34). Read the documentation for this function.

  • Of the SNPs pass that passed the missing.n2 filter (missing.n2 < 0.05), how many SNPs pass/fail the discordance threshold?

  • Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, make a histogram of the discordance rate

  • Make sure you add a new column to snpAnnot called failed.dup.disc (“pass” if the SNP had >=n discordant calls, “fail” if it failed, “untested” if it was one of the high-missingness SNPs that we excluded); update the metadata

#   <Your code goes here>
#Part-6 Qn-1 : Briefly explain the purpose of this step and what it means to check for discordant genotypes. Summarize the results.
#Ans : Checking for discordant genotypes helps spot inconsistencies where the sample has different genotypes at the same SNP. High discordance can indicate genotyping errors, sample mix-ups, or contamination, which may compromise data quality that is why it becomes important to check for duplicate discordance.

#Be sure to answer the following:

#Part-6 Qn-2 : How many SNPs are discordant in at least one duplicate-pair?
#Ans : There are 25 SNPs that are discordant in at least 1 duplicate pair.
nrow(dupdisc$discordance.by.snp[dupdisc$discordance.by.snp$discordant > 0,])
## [1] 25
#Part-6 Qn-3 : What's the maximum number of discordances seen for any SNP?
#Ans : Maximum numer of discordance seen for any SNP is two.
max((dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)$discordant)
## [1] 2
#Part-6 Qn-4 : What's the median discordance rate? What's the maximum discordance rate?
#Ans : The median discordance rate is 0. Maximum discordance rate is 0.07407407.
table((dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)$discord.rate)
## 
##                  0 0.0294117647058824 0.0303030303030303            0.03125 
##               3124                 14                  3                  2 
##  0.032258064516129 0.0344827586206897 0.0666666666666667 0.0740740740740741 
##                  2                  1                  1                  2
median((dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)$discord.rate)
## [1] 0
max((dupdisc$discordance.by.snp)$discord.rate)
## [1] 0.07407407
#Part-6 Qn-5 : Decide and explicitly state the threshold, n, for filtering out SNPs based on discordance (i.e., SNPs with dupdisc$discordance.by.snp >= n will be removed) You may find it helpful to call the function duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34). Read the documentation for this function.
#Since we have 34 pairs, we will remove probe if discordant genotype count is 1.
n_threshold <- duplicateDiscordanceProbability(npair=34)
n_threshold1 <- 1

#Part-6 Qn-6 : Of the SNPs pass that passed the missing.n2 filter (missing.n2 < 0.05), how many SNPs pass/fail the discordance threshold?
merged_data <- dupdisc$discordance.by.snp %>%
  left_join(pData(snpAnnot) %>% select(snpID, missing.n2), by = "snpID")

filtered_snp <- merged_data %>%
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05 & discordant < 1) %>%
  select(snpID)

filtered_snp1 <- merged_data %>%
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05 & discordant >= 1) %>%
  select(snpID)


#Part-6 Qn-7 : Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, make a histogram of the discordance rate
filtered_snp_discord <- merged_data %>%
  filter(missing.n2 < 0.05) %>% 
  select(discord.rate)
hist(
  filtered_snp_discord$discord.rate,
  ylim = c(0, 100),
  xlab = "Discordant rate",
  main = "Histogram of discordant rate when missing.n2 < 0.05"
)

#Part-6 Qn-8 : Make sure you add a new column to snpAnnot called failed.dup.disc ("pass" if the SNP had >=n discordant calls, "fail" if it failed, "untested" if it was one of the high-missingness SNPs that we excluded); update the metadata

pData(snpAnnot) <- pData(snpAnnot) %>%
  mutate(failed.dup.disc = case_when(
    missing.n2 >= 0.05 ~ "untested",  
    discordant >= n_threshold1 ~ "fail",
    discordant < n_threshold1 ~ "pass",  
    TRUE ~ "untested" ))

head(pData(snpAnnot))
##    snpID chromosome position      rsID alleleA alleleB  BeadSetID IntensityOnly
## 1 999447         21 13733610 rs3132407       A       G 1185447327             1
## 2 999465         21 13852569 rs2775671       T       C 1169708488             0
## 3 999493         21 14038583 rs2775018       T       C 1192445330             0
## 4 999512         21 14136579 rs3115511       T       C 1149617207             0
## 5 999561         21 14396024 rs2822404       T       C 1149961944             0
## 6 999567         21 14404476 rs1556276       A       G 1149617207             0
##           tAA       tAB       tBB       rAA       rAB       rBB missing.n1
## 1 0.013743570 0.3290431 0.9184624 1.5622030 1.5927530 1.6142590 1.00000000
## 2 0.063259460 0.5440393 0.9796721 0.4431986 0.4431986 0.4431986 0.01298701
## 3 0.001315146 0.2623954 0.5362323 2.6349810 2.3149340 2.0012760 0.06493506
## 4 0.011004820 0.5692499 0.9846884 0.8781826 0.9453412 0.8209958 0.00000000
## 5 0.040206810 0.5691788 0.9902423 1.0941700 1.1270790 0.9898759 0.00000000
## 6 0.030895730 0.6842008 0.9837771 0.5954081 0.7681253 0.7900150 0.00000000
##   missing.n2 dummy discordant failed.dup.disc
## 1 1.00000000     1         NA        untested
## 2 0.01298701     2          0            pass
## 3 0.06493506     3          0        untested
## 4 0.00000000     4          0            pass
## 5 0.00000000     5          0            pass
## 6 0.00000000     6          0            pass
tail(pData(snpAnnot))
##        snpID chromosome position        rsID alleleA alleleB  BeadSetID
## 3295 1072810         26    16145 MitoT16145C       T       C 1156268751
## 3296 1072812         26    16149 MitoC16149T       T       C 1156268751
## 3297 1072814         26    16164 MitoA16164G       A       G 1156268751
## 3298 1072818         26    16329 MitoC16329T       A       G 1156268751
## 3299 1072819         26    16392 MitoG16392A       T       C 1156268751
## 3300 1072820         26    16393 MitoG16393A       T       C 1156268751
##      IntensityOnly        tAA       tAB       tBB      rAA      rAB      rBB
## 3295             0 0.03779534 0.4693094 0.9339135 3.556648 3.556648 3.556648
## 3296             0 0.03265808 0.4716189 0.9357654 3.323399 3.323399 2.094910
## 3297             0 0.01680967 0.4630159 0.9277538 4.493477 4.493477 2.502799
## 3298             0 0.05817564 0.4740586 0.8851541 6.124516 6.124516 3.724320
## 3299             0 0.04095807 0.4695324 0.9557012 4.793402 4.793402 2.177141
## 3300             0 0.07484803 0.5402368 0.9523321 1.545844 1.545844 1.545844
##      missing.n1 missing.n2 dummy discordant failed.dup.disc
## 3295 0.03896104 0.03896104  3295          0            pass
## 3296 0.02597403 0.02597403  3296          0            pass
## 3297 0.05194805 0.05194805  3297          0        untested
## 3298 0.01298701 0.01298701  3298          0            pass
## 3299 0.02597403 0.02597403  3299          0            pass
## 3300 0.01298701 0.01298701  3300          0            pass
varMetadata(snpAnnot)["failed.dup.disc", "labelDescription"] <-
  paste("SNP status w.r.t missingness and discordant rate")

Part 7 - Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) testing

Deviations from HWE can indicate poor genotyping. Here you will appropriately test for departures from HWE in the data using exactHWE (which applies Fisher’s exact test).

#   The test we'll use for HWE will assume independent observations

#   Therefore we should include only one scan from each subject

#   We should also include only "founders" (for this example, that means excluding the children, whose genotypes aren't independent of their parents')

#   The non-founders are the individuals who have a mother or father in the pedigree

head(pData(scanAnnot)[, c("father", "mother")])
##      father    mother
## 1         0         0
## 2         0         0
## 3 200099417 200191449
## 4 200099417 200191449
## 5         0         0
## 6         0         0
nonfounders <- scanAnnot$father != 0 & scanAnnot$mother != 0
table(nonfounders)
## nonfounders
## FALSE  TRUE 
##    51    26
#   HWE is sensitive to population stratification (A SNP can be in HWE in each of two populations, but out of HWE in the combined population)

#   Therefore in this example we'll focus on HWE in just one population group, the "CEU" group

#   So we'll exclude populations other than CEU, nonfounders, and duplicates
#   (recall tha that the "duplicated" variable was defined to include the scan with better missingness for each subject)

scan.excl <-
  scanAnnot$scanID[scanAnnot$race != "CEU" |
                     nonfounders | scanAnnot$duplicated]
length(scan.excl)
## [1] 60
#   Now we use exactHWE to calculate HWE statistics

#   We do this separately for the sex chromosomes

chr <- getChromosome(genoData)
auto <- range(which(chr %in% 1:22))
X <- range(which(chr == 23))

#   exactHWE takes snpStart/snpEnd arguments; we use these to identify the
#   indices corresponding to autosomal/sex chromosome SNPs

hwe <-
  exactHWE(
    genoData,
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snpStart = auto[1],
    snpEnd = auto[2]
  )
## Beginning Calculations...
## Block 1 of 1 Completed - 0.00536 secs
hweX <-
  exactHWE(
    genoData,
    scan.exclude = scan.excl,
    snpStart = X[1],
    snpEnd = X[2]
  )
## Beginning Calculations...
## Block 1 of 1 Completed - 0.001462 secs
#   We use the scan.exclude argument to exclude non-CEU, non-founders, and duplicates

#   exactHWE has returned a data frame of HWE statistics for each SNP; read the help page to see what the columns are

#   Attach the two data frames

hwe <- rbind(hwe, hweX)
names(hwe)
## [1] "snpID"        "chr"          "nAA"          "nAB"          "nBB"         
## [6] "MAF"          "minor.allele" "f"            "pval"
#close(genoData)

#   Check on sample sizes for autosomes

hwe$N <- hwe$nAA + hwe$nAB + hwe$nBB
summary(hwe$N[is.element(hwe$chr, 1:22)])
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    0.00   17.00   17.00   16.93   17.00   17.00
#   ... and for the X chromosome

summary(hwe$N[is.element(hwe$chr, 23)])
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   0.000   9.000   9.000   8.547   9.000   9.000
#   Notice there are some missing p-values for the autosomes

#   ... and for X

#   We can view some of the genotype counts

#   The first SNP appears to be 100% missing, and the second is monomorphic
#   (i.e., MAF=0)

#   (recall that we didn't filter out SNPs here)

hwe$pval[1:10]
##  [1]        NA        NA 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000 0.5372636 0.1788856
##  [8] 1.0000000 1.0000000 1.0000000
sum(is.na(hwe$pval[hwe$chr == 23]))
## [1] 299
head(hwe[, c("nAA", "nAB", "nBB")])
##   nAA nAB nBB
## 1   0   0   0
## 2   0   0  17
## 3   0   4  11
## 4   1   7   9
## 5   0   2  15
## 6  11   5   1
#   Now we want to inspect deviation from HWE

#   We can use Q-Q plots to help with this

#   We should exclude monomorphic SNPs

hwe.0 <- hwe[hwe$MAF > 0, ]
dim(hwe.0)
## [1] 2540   10
#   Only keep the autosomal SNPs for first plot

pval <- hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 1:22)]
length(pval)
## [1] 1785
#   Double-check that there are no missing p-values remaining

pval <- pval[!is.na(pval)]
length(pval)
## [1] 1785
#   Similarly, get the p-values for the X-chromosome

pval.x <- hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 23)]
length(pval.x)
## [1] 701
pval.x <- pval.x[!is.na(pval.x)]
length(pval.x)
## [1] 701
#   Now make Q-Q plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
qqPlot(pval = pval,
       truncate = FALSE,
       main = "Autosomes, all")
qqPlot(pval = pval,
       truncate = TRUE,
       main = "Autosomes, truncated")
qqPlot(pval = pval.x,
       truncate = FALSE,
       main = "X chromosome, all")
qqPlot(pval = pval.x,
       truncate = TRUE,
       main = "X chromosome, truncated")

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Part 7 - Report

Briefly explain the purpose of HWE testing. In describing your results for this step, answer the folowing:

  • How do you choose what subjects to include for HWE testing? How many were used here?

  • Interpret the Q-Q plots. Do any SNPs appear to be strongly out of HWE? Which SNPs? What are the lowest p-values?

  • For the autosomal SNPs, make a plot of -log10(p-value) vs MAF. Make another such plot for the X chromosome.

  • What p-value will you use for filtering our SNPs with low HWE? Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, how many will be retained/removed?

  • Add the HWE p-values to the SNP annotation (and update the metadata)

#   <Your code goes here>
#Part-7 Qn-1 : Briefly explain the purpose of HWE testing. In describing your results for this step, answer the folowing:
#Ans : We use HWE testing in QC to detect genotyping errors, population stratification and natural selection effects.

#Part-7 Qn-2 : How do you choose what subjects to include for HWE testing? How many were used here?
#Ans : We need only founders and same population since we dont need stratified population and we remove duplicates as well. After all this we get 17 subject IDs.
subj_ID <- pData(scanAnnot) %>% 
  filter(father == 0 & mother == 0 & race == "CEU" & duplicated(subjectID) == FALSE) %>% 
  select(subjectID)
nrow(subj_ID)
## [1] 17
#Part-7 Qn-3 : Interpret the Q-Q plots. Do any SNPs appear to be strongly out of HWE? Which SNPs? What are the lowest p-values?
#Ans : The lowest p-value is 5.616715e-05. The threshold value I have taken is 1e-4 since that is often recommended. Based on plot, I could see one outlier and after setting the threshold value, the outlier goes away confirming the threshold value. The snp which was removed was 1015218
pval_noNA <- hwe %>% 
  filter(!is.na(pval))
  
snp_retained <- pval_noNA$snpID[pval_noNA$pval > 1e-4]
snp_removed <-  pval_noNA$snpID[pval_noNA$pval <= 1e-4] 


min_pval <- hwe %>%
  filter(!is.na(pval)) %>%
  summarize(min_pval = min(pval, na.rm = TRUE))
min_pval
##       min_pval
## 1 5.616715e-05
#Part-7 Qn-4 : For the autosomal SNPs, make a plot of -log10(p-value) vs MAF. Make another such plot for the X chromosome.
plot(hwe.0$MAF[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 1:22)], -log10(hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 1:22)]),
     xlab = "Minor Allele Frequency (MAF)", 
     ylab = "-log10(HWE p-value)",
     main = "Autosomal SNPs: HWE vs MAF",
     pch = 20, col = "blue")

plot(hwe.0$MAF[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 23)], -log10(hwe.0$pval[is.element(hwe.0$chr, 23)]),
     xlab = "Minor Allele Frequency (MAF)", 
     ylab = "-log10(HWE p-value)",
     main = "X Chromosome SNPs: HWE vs MAF",
     pch = 20, col = "red")

#Part-7 Qn-5 : What p-value will you use for filtering our SNPs with low HWE? Of the SNPs with missing.n2 < 0.05, how many will be retained/removed?
#Ans : I used 1e-4 for filtering SNPs with low HWE. 
merged_df2 <- left_join(hwe, pData(snpAnnot), by = "snpID")

final_snp_retained <- merged_df2 %>% 
  filter(!is.na(pval) & missing.n2 < 0.05) %>% 
  filter(pval > 1e-4)

final_snp_removed <- merged_df2 %>% 
  filter(!is.na(pval) & missing.n2 < 0.05) %>% 
  filter(pval <= 1e-4)


#Part-7 Qn-6 : Add the HWE p-values to the SNP annotation (and update the metadata)
#Ans : 
pData(snpAnnot) <- pData(snpAnnot) %>% 
  mutate(pval <- left_join(.,hwe, by = "snpID"))

varMetadata(snpAnnot)["pval", "labelDescription"] <- "Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium's p-value"

ACKNOWLEDGEMENT : This work was done is parallel with Laskhita Arunkumar. However, I take ultimate responsibility for the content of this assignment.
